serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately one sandwich (200 g), consisting of a crispy French baguette filled with two sunny-side up fried eggs and fresh herbs.

nutrient_source.title: Egg sandwich / fried egg on bread — composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Egg, whole, cooked, fried (NDB 01128) and French bread (NDB 18033), weighted for typical serving composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct HPB Singapore or USDA entry exists for Banh Mi Op La. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy components: fried egg (NDB 01128) at approximately 40% by weight and French baguette (NDB 18033) at approximately 50% by weight, with 10% fresh herbs and condiments. Values are weighted per 100 g of the assembled sandwich. This is a medium-confidence composite estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white bread / baguette reference; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Banh Mi Op La. GI of 70 is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate component being a white wheat baguette, which has a published GI of approximately 70 (white bread reference, University of Sydney GI Database). The egg component does not contribute meaningfully to GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 x 53.0) / 100 = 37.1, where carbohydrate per 200 g serving = 53.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 380 mg per 100 g, yielding approximately 760 mg per sandwich serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this sandwich as one serving provides approximately 760 mg sodium, representing about one-third of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; choosing lower-sodium condiments and reducing added soy sauce can help mitigate sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index of 70 and high glycemic load of 37.1 per serving driven by white wheat baguette
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this sandwich with caution due to its high GI (70) and high glycemic load (37.1 per serving); pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables or choosing a wholegrain bread alternative can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content approximately 185 mg per 100 g from egg yolks, with moderate saturated fat at 2.4 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of egg yolk cholesterol (approximately 370 mg per sandwich serving) and moderate saturated fat content; current evidence suggests dietary cholesterol from eggs has modest impact on LDL in most individuals, but frequency of consumption should be discussed with a clinician.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Banh Mi Op La (Bánh Mì Ốp La) is a Vietnamese-origin fried egg sandwich served on a crispy French baguette, popularised across Southeast Asia including Singapore. The dish consists of one or two sunny-side up or over-easy fried eggs placed inside or atop a split, lightly toasted Vietnamese-style baguette, accompanied by fresh herbs such as coriander and spring onion, and optionally seasoned with soy sauce, Maggi seasoning, or chilli sauce. The baguette is characteristically light and airy inside with a thin, shatteringly crisp crust, a legacy of French colonial influence on Vietnamese cuisine. The eggs are typically pan-fried in butter or oil until the whites are set and lightly crisped at the edges while the yolk remains runny. Nutritionally, one sandwich (approximately 200 g) provides around 436 kcal, 18.4 g protein, 17 g fat, and 53 g carbohydrate. The high glycemic index (GI 70) driven by the white baguette makes this a high-GL meal, warranting caution for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on condiments used. The dish is a common breakfast or light meal option in Vietnamese restaurants and Vietnamese-influenced eateries in Singapore, including establishments such as Co Chung Restaurant.

confidence_reason: No direct HPB Singapore or USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Banh Mi Op La as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for fried egg and French baguette weighted by typical serving composition. GI is estimated by analogy to white baguette from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation rather than direct measurement.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy: fried egg (NDB 01128) and French bread (NDB 18033), weighted at approximately 40% egg and 50% baguette by mass per 100 g assembled sandwich. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and are not directly measured for this dish; these values carry higher uncertainty. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white baguette reference from the University of Sydney GI Database. No sentinel -1 values remain unexplained; all micronutrient values are estimated from USDA component sources as documented above. The dish is served at Co Chung Restaurant, Lorong Telok, Singapore, as sourced from field survey data (price SGD 16.48). Vietnamese multilingual name Bánh Mì Ốp La is the canonical Vietnamese form. Singaporean cuisine classification applied as the dish is served in Singapore context; Vietnamese origin is noted in region_of_origin.
